familiar with this analysis, this is the
uh probably the the siminal conclusion
from our uh uh uh business cycle. Uh we
did a deep dive study on on the US
business cycle. So I'm not a big fan of
uh academic economist theories and
goblygook. I like to look at empirical
data. If it's not observable in the
data, then it doesn't exist in my
opinion. Uh you know, I don't believe in
theories. I believe in empirics. And so
we said, okay, well, how does the
business cycle actually work? I don't
care what the books tell me. what has
actually occurred in the economy. And so
we did a deep dive study to analyze
hundreds of time series to identify okay
how do they you know which ones lead
which ones lag which ones acrew incident
with the business cycle. And so the key
takeaway the one the main takeaway from
that study there's plenty of takeaways
but this is the big one is that there is
a pattern to which how the business
cycle works. It's not you know we don't
just pop into an expansion or pop into a
contraction. We don't go to recession.
We don't go to recover. You know it's
not random. there's a sequence to how
the business cycle was historically
worked. So, uh, what I'm showing here is
the median trailing 10-year delta
adjusted zcore for each of these cycles
that comprise the business cycle.
There's a bunch of cycles within the
economy that comprise the business
cycle. And so, essentially what that
that sounds mathy, what it essentially
is, it's the median path that each of
these cycles takes into and through a
recession. And so, how it the business
cycles historically worked and there's
there's 12 postwar business cycles that
represent the median for each of these
cycles. So policy tends to get
restrictive. It's called 15 18 months
ahead of a recession. And corporate
profits break down 12 months ahead of a
recession. Then liquidity breaks down
three quarters ahead of a recession.
Then growth in stocks break down
simultaneously two quarters ahead of a
recession. Then the unemployment rate
breaks out right when the recession
starts. Uh so again everything's delta
adjusted. So down is bad for the
business cycle. Up is good. Uh
unemployment rate breaks out right when
the recession starts. That's when the
MBR noticed uh that's when the baggie
suit baggie suit wearing economist
noticed and they put the red bar on the
chart uh after the fact uh uh and and so
and then credit break delinquency charge
offs break out uh quarter after the
recession starts and then inflation the
most lagging indicator of the business
cycle breaks down 12 to 15 months after
a recession is already started. Jay if
you're waiting for inflation to break
down we will have gone through a
recession already.

supply. So obviously that's

latest value is 4.5%. It's applying a

so M2 divided by nominal GDP, that
latest value is 1.4 as a ratio. That's
applying a modest amount of uh
disinflationary pressure. If we look at
monopsin power, so S&P 500, S&P 100
market cap divided by S&P 500 market

100 companies in the S&P 500 represent
72% of the market cap. That's applying a
meaningful amount of of inflationary
pressure. So essentially the more you
know monopsinities you have the more
pricing power they have and the more
pricing power they have the more they
can raise prices. And so that's adding a
meaningful amount of inflationary
pressure. We look at populism. So we
look at the employment to population
ratio of the 25 to 54. The prime working
age population uh club to population
ratio at 81% is applying a a modest
amount of disinflationary pressure. And
so um you know as that you know as that
goes uh up you you're essentially saying
you know with sorry as the employment to
population ratio goes down you're going
to get more populism. So it's now it's
gone up you know it's actually gone uh
it's actually I think it's at all time
high so it's applying disinflationary
pressure. Uh productivity if you look at
output per hour so of non-farm that the
productivity growth is uh 1.3% on a
year-over-year basis that's essentially
a wash with regards to inflation. public
debt. If you look at federal debt held
by the public as a percentage of GDP,
the latest value is 106%. That's
applying a meaningful amount of
inflationary pressure. You look at
wages, the employment cost index, the
latest value there is 3.6%
year-over-year. That's applying a
meaningful amount of inflationary
pressure. And then the West Village
Monttok effect. Uh so uh for those who
may be new, the West Village Monttok
effect is a phenomenon that we
identified in the economy back in 2023
uh to support our Brazilian US economy.
Well, we didn't we identified it and
recognized that it would support our
resilient US economy that even has for a
long period of time. And so what the
West Village Montto effect is is
essentially uh the we have so much we
have such a high stock of of of savings
on the household sector balance sheets
as a function of the uh the historic
fiscal and monetary large we've seen
since co uh you know the the the
checkable deposits and currency as a
percent of total household assets is now
3% of total household assets. the
highest percentage we've ever seen in
the time series data going all the way
back to the 50s that's applying the most
amount of inflationary pressure and the
reason why is because we have all this
cash sloshing around in people's
checking accounts that can go be spent
into the economy at any given time. So
this is part of the reason why the
economies have been resilient and and
the and the origin of this you know the
reason I was able to notice this and and
you know without a you know academic
economist you know PhD you know the the
econ don't even realize this but the
reason I was able to notice this because
I'm someone who you know spent a lot of
time partying in West Village and you
realize you know that the people who
spend the most money in these places at
Surf Lodge and Common Ground people who
spend the most money aren't the richest
people it's the people whose mom and
dads are the richest because they can
spend a higher share of their income
into the economy week after week, month
after month. If your mom and daddy
already saved all the money that you're
going to need to retire with, you can
take 100% of your Wall Street analyst or
associate income and spend it on bottle
service. But people like me who have to,
you know, send money back home and and
actually grow a nest egg for my family,
I can't spend all my money on bottle
service because I actually have to save
money. And so the reverse of that is
true as well. is if you already have a
bunch of money in the bank, you don't
need to keep saving money. And so that's
why the savings rate has been
structurally depressed throughout this
entire business cycle. That's why
consumer spending has been relatively
resilient throughout this business cycle
because the relatively resilient
certainly relatively elevated policy
rate and that's because we have all this
these savings on the house sector
balance sheets. Now it's obviously not
even evenly distributed. You know, it's
obviously we have a big K-shaped economy
and the top part of the K, you know, if
you look at the upper median, the top
third of consumers account for 51% of
consumer spending, whereas the bottom
third of consumers only account for 15%
of consumer spending. So, uh, you know,
it's a very unevenly distributed K. Uh,
but the reality is that was all
contributing to uh the resiliency of the
economy. But this is contributing to a
high level of inflation. And so, the key
takeaway is that this is an economy
based on all these factors. I've been
talking for 10 minutes here. all these
factors. The economy just kind of wants
to have high twos, low threes inflation,
but the Fed has this arbitrary 2%
inflation target that it got from some
dead central banker in New Zealand.
And so, of course, they're going to run
monetary policy too tight because
they're trying to get to a level of
inflation that is quite frankly asinine
in the context of everything I've been
talking about. And so uh and and the and
the only way you can really win here is
if you're the federal reserve, you know,
they can get lucky on the productivity
boom because uh what I did in the model
is I said okay on the unweighted models
or sorry weight all the factors evenly,
the economy wants to have 2.9% uh cc
inflation on the equilibrium basis. But
if we weight AI for 50% of the weight
and then give all the the the remainder
the the residual to all the other
factors on an equal basis. So AI is now
50% of the model. That's how you get to
an equilibrium core PC inflation level
of 2%. So we literally have to have a
disinflationary productivity boom for
the inflation to to durably rest at the
Fed's 2% target without a recession. So
if that's the outcome they're betting
on, they're going to make a policy
mistake because otherwise,
this why I've been saying, you got to
let the the the the they got to change
the inflation target. The inflation
target has to be three. If you keep it
at two, it's going to create negative
outcomes. That's part of the reason why
we have a K-shaped economy. Oh, by the
way, everybody that goes Darius that
they revised the inflation target to 3%,
won't that destroy the bond market? And
I'll say the exact same thing to you
that Mariner Eckleys and Thomas McCabe
said to the bond vigilantes back then,
which is nope. You're going out of
business. I'm going to buy every bond in
sight that's above 2.5% yield, which is
what they did for nine years. The Fed
could easily say, we're going to cap the
10-year Treasury yield at 3.5% and every
bond on Earth that's trading above 3.5%
is going to be bought and sold to the
Fed. You don't think there's going to I
will start a hedge fund to buy every
Treasury on Earth to sell to the Fed if
they're going to cap yields at some
point, which is exactly where I think
we're headed over the long term. It may
not get there over the next few weeks or
months or the next couple of years, but
that's where we're headed by the end of
this fourth turning poly crisis. I
guarantee it. So, uh, we'll wrap it up
there. Terry Stale here presenting our
round the horn for Saturday, August 2nd,
2025. Uh, best of luck out there. Uh,
actually, have a great weekend.
Remember, if we're starting to correct,
just think about this shirt. Go to the
beach. We're still in paradigm C. Unless
you hear me say we're going back to
paradigm B, you got to assume the dip
will be bought. And so, we'll catch you
back here next week. Have a great
